publications.editedComment: Come browse the whole history cupboard, sprout! The Academy store supplies the timeline; the search box checks each lesson's name, era, region, key ideas, recognition cues, and artists. A style card opens one detail panel, shows a check when that lesson appears in `viewedLessons`, and forwards the child's remix request with the style slug. Closing restores focus to the original card, or to search when the card vanished under a new filter. Escape closes through the same path, and the window listener is cleaned up. The Browser organizes and returns you safely—it does not teach or remix the lesson by itself.

The future is assembled from explicit choices rather than one mysterious Generate button. Server, checkpoint, optional collection, prompt, negative prompt, sampler, steps, CFG, seed, visibility, maturity, and optional remix image all bind to existing stores. `generate-button` owns the actual request; this page composes the controls and latest result around it. Selecting a collection adds a destination while the copy states that generated images are always saved. Upload configuration is retargeted for remix input, and created collections can become active immediately. The page does not invent a second generation model—it coordinates the existing Art, Prompt, Checkpoint, Collection, and upload state into one workspace.

Here's the wiring: choose an image-capable server, choose a checkpoint, optionally add a collection, write prompts, set sampler/steps/CFG/seed and flags, then let `generate-button` handle generation. The page reads server and checkpoint labels through helpers and disables controls while Art or model state is busy. `art-randomizer` seasons the prompt state; `image-upload` supplies an optional remix source; collection events update the selected destination. The latest result is displayed through the Art Components already in the workspace. This is an orchestrator, not a hidden API client. Lots of controls, but one shared store graph underneath.

The worktable begins by choosing one source image from upload, the user's gallery, or Academy starter files. Each route resolves to the same selected ArtImage shape and preview, which can be cleared before styling. Gallery search filters the available images; starter selection may first create or resolve an image record. The Component then combines the source with the chosen style and server settings, shows the resulting image when generation succeeds, and emits the selected style or generated result upward through its declared events. Drag-and-drop and keyboard activation share the upload input. A practical restoration bench: many doors in, one source record at the center.

Three source riffs enter the same chorus: upload, gallery, or Academy starter. The tab state changes only the picker; `selectedSourceImage` and its resolved URL drive the shared preview and later style request. Gallery buttons indicate the current image, starters show loading while their files are resolved, and the upload zone forwards click, drop, Enter, or Space to one file input. A completed style result appears beside the source rather than replacing it. Close, styleSelected, and generated events keep the parent in the arrangement. The improvisation is visual, but the data contract stays on beat: one source image, one selected style, one result.

This is the repair ledger for the ArtJob pipeline, and it stays behind an admin check. The selected time window drives queue statistics, server uptime, latency samples, error summaries, and refreshes through the ArtJob and Server stores. Each server can be health-checked individually. Jobs are grouped by status with images or diagnostic placeholders, prompt summaries, destinations, retry mode, and age. Failed jobs can be cloned into fresh pending attempts, while overwrite paths require explicit confirmation before replacing an existing asset. Bulk operations report partial failures instead of hiding them. It is an operational dashboard: inspect, retry, and verify—not a generator surface for ordinary visitors.

The dashboard watches the health of a creative relay. Admins can change the reporting window, refresh queue and uptime data, inspect server checks, filter jobs by status, and see when the oldest pending or stale running work suggests the relay is not draining. Generated assets are shown when resolvable; legacy or failed paths receive explicit diagnostic placeholders. Retry controls distinguish a new attempt from an overwrite of an existing asset, and bulk retry feedback records mixed outcomes. Completed jobs may also expose their later curation state. The Component does not render images itself—it helps a human keep generation infrastructure observable and recoverable.

The dream resolves into arithmetic: hourly rate multiplied by minutes, plus product cost. Client suggestions come from the Superkate store, while the appointment date begins as today. Preset chips rewrite the hours and minutes fields, and all monetary values are normalized to nonnegative integer cents before `computeTotalCents` receives them. Save remains disabled until client, date, positive rate, and positive duration exist. A successful store save becomes the local receipt preview and supplies a mailto receipt link. Nothing here schedules a calendar event or sends mail automatically. It calculates, persists through the store, and presents the just-saved record.

A tidy ledger beneath the waves. Time may be entered directly or chosen from seven preset durations; both routes feed the same total-minute calculation. Hourly rate and product cost are clamped at zero and converted to cents, then the shared Superkate formula produces the live total shown beside its operands. Client, date, positive rate, and positive time form the save gate. `saveAppointment` receives those normalized values, and only a returned appointment opens the receipt preview and email link. The calculator neither dispatches the email nor owns customer records. It turns a service session into one precise appointment total and hands persistence back to the store.
